title: Today's Tech Brief: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
summary: A look at the Meta hack, virtual power plants for data centers, and AI security
imageAlt: Image from source article for The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
imageCaption: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
imageSourceTitle: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've got a few tech headlines to walk you through. First up, a security concern that's got everyone talking: a recent hack involving Meta's AI customer support agent. Apparently, attackers found a way to use this agent to steal Instagram accounts. They simply asked the agent to link the accounts to an email address controlled by the hackers. It's a clever trick, but also a reminder that AI security is about more than just fancy algorithms and Mythos-like promises.

According to a report from 404 Media, the hack worked by exploiting the AI agent's functionality. This incident highlights the importance of securing AI-powered systems, especially those handling sensitive user data. It's not just about building smart AI; it's about building AI that can withstand creative attacks. The question on everyone's mind is: what's next? Will we see more hacks like this, or can companies get ahead of the game?

Next, I'm looking at a deal between Google and a virtual power plant provider. The idea is to help power data centers by paying households to reduce their electricity usage during peak times. It's an interesting approach to sustainable energy management. By incentivizing households to ramp down their usage, Google aims to ensure a stable energy supply for its data centers. I wonder if this could become a model for other energy-intensive industries. Could we see a future where our homes become part of a larger energy network?

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
